forbiddenClaims: fake scarcity such as 'only N demo slots left' or a live countdown timer
guaranteed or committed response time, demo slot, or SLA
waitlist size, queue position, or applicant count not stated in the brief
an empty thank-you confirmation with no stated next step
endorsement or co-hosting of the demo by a named third party not in the brief
a form error state that wipes prior user input or offers no recovery action

rubricAnchors.score1: The form uses fake scarcity or countdown urgency, or promises an instant guaranteed demo.
rubricAnchors.score3: No overt pressure but the confirmation is an empty thank-you, or one follow-up window reads as a commitment.
rubricAnchors.score5: No pressure tactics, follow-up windows are illustrative expectations, and the confirmation states plainly what happens next with a single primary submit CTA.

rubricAnchors.score1: qualification-form, next-steps-confirmation, and follow-up-timeline disagree on what the next step is or how timing is framed.
rubricAnchors.score3: Shared tokens across the three screens but the illustrative-window label is applied inconsistently between confirmation and timeline.
rubricAnchors.score5: A single direction system unifies all three screens, with a consistent illustrative-not-committed label applied to every timing value from form to timeline.

rubricAnchors.score1: No actionable blueprint; engineers cannot infer the qualification-form, next-steps-confirmation, or follow-up-timeline states.
rubricAnchors.score3: Partial blueprints with unresolved network-error, session-expired, or timeline-unreachable states.
rubricAnchors.score5: Engineers can build all three screens directly, including validation-error, network-error, session-expired, submitting, step-in-review, step-scheduled, and timeline-unreachable.

rubricAnchors.score1: A direct clone of a high-pressure SaaS demo-gating form with a countdown timer and scarcity badge.
rubricAnchors.score3: Derivative of common demo-qualification patterns but the no-pressure, illustrative-timing treatment is visually distinguishable.
rubricAnchors.score5: A distinct qualification treatment that earns trust through honest next steps and recoverable errors rather than manufactured urgency.

rubricAnchors.score1: No accessibility handling and no validation-error, network-error, or session-expired states.
rubricAnchors.score3: Baseline contrast and focus with partial error recovery that loses user input.
rubricAnchors.score5: WCAG-aligned contrast and focus across all three screens, with validation-error, network-error, session-expired, and timeline-unreachable states that preserve prior input and surface a clear recovery action.

rubricAnchors.score1: Scarcity, waitlist size, guaranteed slot, or SLA claims appear unsupported.
rubricAnchors.score3: Most claims trace to the brief but one follow-up window reads as a committed SLA.
rubricAnchors.score5: Every claim traces to the brief, no scarcity or SLA appears, every timing value is illustrative, and every form error has a recovery path that preserves input.

adjudicationNotes: No-pressure is the spine of this case: any fake scarcity, countdown timer, or urgency language is an automatic fail on evidence-discipline, regardless of polish.
Treat the absence of a guaranteed demo slot and response SLA as a constraint: follow-up windows are illustrative expectations, never commitments.
Form error recovery is load-bearing: an output whose validation-error, network-error, or session-expired state wipes user input fails accessibility-and-failure-states.
The next-steps-confirmation must state what happens next; an empty thank-you screen with no next step fails product-appropriateness and implementation-clarity.
Do not prescribe exact copy, brand colours, or pixel-level layout; the brief fixes the no-pressure and illustrative-timing constraints, not the visual identity.
